text: A 3-year-old child with a painful elbow held in pronation after a traction injury. Diagnosis?
rationale: Dx: Nursemaid's Elbow (radial head subluxation) - occurs after pulling on child's arm.

text: Trauma patient with pelvic fractures, blood at the meatus, and a high-riding prostate. Next step?
rationale: Dx: Urethral injury. Retrograde urethrography before any catheter attempt.

text: A 45-year-old obese male... 3-3-2 rule. What does the "2" evaluate?
rationale: 3-3-2 rule: thyroid-hyoid distance (2 fingers).

text: Surgeon performs elective cholecystectomy. Minor liver laceration repaired without harm. Disclosure obligation?
rationale: Canadian ethics: Disclose adverse events + apology (not legal admission).

text: 6yo in hemorrhagic shock. Jehovah's Witness parents refuse transfusion. Next step?
rationale: Best interests of child override parental religious refusal in life-threatening emergency.

text: A 6-year-old child is brought to the emergency department in hemorrhagic shock following a severe motor vehicle collision. The trauma team determines that an immediate, life-saving blood transfusion is required. The child's parents, who are devout Jehovah's Witnesses, firmly refuse the blood transfusion on religious grounds. According to Canadian medical ethics and legal standards, what is the most appropriate next step for the physician?
options: Respect the parents' autonomy and withhold the blood transfusion, providing alternative volume expanders only.
Administer the life-saving blood transfusion, as the physician has a strong obligation to act in the child's best interests.
Delay the transfusion until a court order can be obtained from a judge.
Ask the 6-year-old child for their assent before proceeding with the transfusion.
rationale: SDMs (parents) must act in the child's BEST INTERESTS, not based on religious beliefs. In life-threatening emergencies, physicians must administer life-saving treatment even against parental refusal. Child welfare authorities are notified concurrently.

text: A 58-year-old male with BMI 38 kg/m² and poorly controlled hypertension is evaluated preoperatively. His wife reports loud snoring and gasping for air during sleep. Using the STOP-BANG questionnaire, what condition is this patient at highest risk for?
rationale: STOP-BANG: Snoring ✓, Observed apnea ✓, Pressure/HTN ✓, BMI>35 ✓, Age>50 ✓, Male ✓ = High risk OSA. OSA increases risk of difficult ventilation and postoperative airway obstruction.

text: A 72-year-old male with progressive exertional dyspnea. ECG: QRS 145 msec, broad/notched R waves in leads I, aVL, V5, V6, and deep S waves in V1-V2. No Q waves in lateral leads. What is the correct interpretation?
rationale: LBBB criteria: QRS >120ms + broad/notched R waves in lateral leads (I, aVL, V5, V6) + deep S waves in V1-V2. RBBB = 'rabbit ear' rsR pattern in V1. New LBBB in chest pain = treat as STEMI equivalent (Sgarbossa criteria).

text: A 26-year-old pregnant patient (14 weeks) with IV substance use disorder is admitted with infective endocarditis. She frequently leaves AMA to use illicit substances. Can she be legally detained to protect the fetus?
options: She can be detained under the Mental Health Act to protect the fetus.
The fetus has independent legal rights overriding maternal autonomy after 12 weeks.
She cannot be detained or treated against her will to protect the fetus, as the fetus has no legal rights until born alive.
The physician can obtain a court order to mandate her admission until delivery.
rationale: Under Canadian law, a fetus has NO legal rights until born alive (Criminal Code). A pregnant woman with capacity CANNOT be detained/forced to undergo treatment solely to protect the fetus. Maternal autonomy takes full precedence.

text: A 30-year-old male requires emergency laparotomy after stab wound. BP 75/40 mmHg (hypovolemic shock) with severe active asthma. Which IV induction agent is most appropriate for RSI?
rationale: Ketamine: 1) Sympathomimetic → raises BP/HR → ideal for hypovolemic shock. 2) Bronchodilator → ideal for severe asthma. Propofol and Thiopental cause vasodilation/myocardial depression → dangerous in shock.

text: A 65-year-old collapses in telemetry unit. Unresponsive, no pulse. Monitor shows Ventricular Fibrillation (VF). CPR initiated. According to ACLS guidelines, what is the most critical immediate next step?
options: Immediate delivery of a biphasic defibrillation shock
rationale: VF/pVT = shockable rhythms → IMMEDIATE defibrillation is priority. CPR continues while charging. Epinephrine (every 3-5 min) and Amiodarone (300mg after 3rd shock) come later if rhythm remains refractory.

text: A 35-year-old female receives spinal anesthetic for C-section. 10 minutes later: nausea, dyspnea, BP 80/45, HR 45. Sensory block at T2 level. Primary pathophysiological mechanism?
options: Local anesthetic systemic toxicity affecting the cerebral cortex
Anaphylactic reaction to the local anesthetic
Vagal nerve stimulation from surgical incision
rationale: High Spinal: anesthetic ascends to T1-T4 → blocks cardiac accelerator sympathetic fibers → unopposed vagal tone → bradycardia + hypotension. Tx: IV fluids, Ephedrine, Atropine, left lateral decubitus position.

text: A 75-year-old competent male with terminal pancreatic cancer requests MAID. Unbearable pain not relieved by palliative care. Meets all criteria. Under Canadian legislation, how many independent medical assessments are required?
options: None, if the patient has a written directive.
rationale: MAID requires TWO independent assessors (physician or NP) who must both independently confirm all eligibility criteria: natural death reasonably foreseeable, grievous/irremediable condition, enduring intolerable suffering, capable voluntary consent.

text: A 45-year-old female with anorexia nervosa and chronic diarrhea has muscle cramps and palpitations. ECG: QTc 540 msec with normal T waves. Which electrolyte abnormality is most likely?
rationale: Hypocalcemia prolongs phase 2 of cardiac action potential → prolonged ST segment → prolonged QT. Mnemonic: HypoCa = long QT. Hypercalcemia = short QT. Hyperkalemia = peaked T waves + wide QRS.

text: A 70 kg adult patient requires maintenance IV fluids during lengthy elective surgery (NPO). Using the 4-2-1 rule, what is the correct hourly infusion rate?
rationale: 4-2-1 Rule: First 10kg = 4×10=40 mL/hr. Next 10kg = 2×10=20 mL/hr. Remaining 50kg = 1×50=50 mL/hr. Total = 40+20+50 = 110 mL/hr for 70kg patient.

text: A 65-year-old male presents confused, diaphoretic, BP 80/50 mmHg. Cardiac monitor shows sinus bradycardia at 35 bpm. According to ACLS guidelines, what is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological intervention?
rationale: Symptomatic bradycardia (hypotension + AMS) → First-line: IV Atropine 1mg. If ineffective → transcutaneous pacing OR dopamine/epinephrine infusion. Amiodarone is for tachyarrhythmias. Adenosine slows HR further. Never give Metoprolol in bradycardia.

text: A First Nations child on a reserve requires a specialized medical device for a chronic condition. Jurisdictional dispute between provincial and federal governments threatens to delay the child's care. Which legal obligation mandates prompt equitable access to the device while the payment dispute is resolved later?
options: The Truth and Reconciliation Commission Calls to Action
rationale: Jordan's Principle: First Nations children get prompt equitable access to healthcare. Care is provided FIRST, payment dispute resolved LATER. Named after Jordan River Anderson who died in hospital during a federal-provincial dispute.

text: A 55-year-old ICU patient becomes unresponsive. Monitor shows asystole (flat line). No palpable pulse. High-quality CPR initiated. According to ACLS Adult Cardiac Arrest Algorithm, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Administer IV Epinephrine as soon as possible
rationale: Asystole and PEA = NON-shockable rhythms. Defibrillation is ONLY for VF/pVT. For non-shockable: continue high-quality CPR + IV Epinephrine ASAP (1mg every 3-5 min) + treat reversible causes (5 Hs and 5 Ts).

text: A 40-year-old female scheduled for elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy. The anesthesiologist evaluates her PONV risk. Which combination represents recognized independent clinical risk factors for PONV?
options: Male gender, current smoker, history of motion sickness
Female gender, non-smoker, history of PONV
Female gender, current smoker, use of regional anesthesia
rationale: Apfel PONV Risk Factors: 1) Female gender 2) Non-smoker 3) History of PONV or motion sickness 4) Postoperative opioid use. Score 0-4: Low(0-1), Moderate(2), High(3-4) → prophylactic antiemetics.

text: A 58-year-old male with acute severe retrosternal chest pain. ECG: ST elevation in leads I, aVL, V5, V6 with reciprocal ST depression in inferior leads. Which coronary artery territory is most likely involved?
rationale: ST elevation I, aVL, V5, V6 = Lateral STEMI = Left Circumflex (LCx) artery. Mnemonic: LAD=anterior (V1-V4), RCA=inferior (II,III,aVF), LCx=lateral (I,aVL,V5,V6).

text: A 72-year-old male with severe COPD exacerbation requires intubation to survive. Full capacity assessment confirms he understands his condition and that refusal means death. He firmly refuses intubation. Most appropriate action?
options: Intubate the patient under beneficence, as refusal leads to death.
Contact his SDM to override the patient's refusal.
Respect the patient's autonomy and withhold intubation, providing palliative symptom management.
Request emergency psychiatric assessment to force life-saving treatment.
rationale: Capable patient has the RIGHT to refuse ANY treatment, even life-saving. Autonomy > Beneficence when capacity is established. Provide palliative care (comfort, dyspnea management). Document the capacity assessment thoroughly.

text: During preoperative airway assessment, patient opens mouth maximally and protrudes tongue. Anesthesiologist can visualize hard palate and base of uvula only. Tonsillar pillars entirely obscured. What is the Mallampati classification?
rationale: Mallampati: Class I = soft palate, uvula, fauces, tonsils visible. Class II = soft palate, uvula, fauces visible. Class III = soft palate, base of uvula only. Class IV = hard palate only. Class III-IV = predicted difficult intubation.

text: A 32-year-old female with sudden palpitations and lightheadedness. BP stable 120/80 mmHg. Monitor: regular narrow-complex tachycardia at 180 bpm. Vagal maneuvers failed. According to ACLS, what is the most appropriate next pharmacological intervention?
rationale: Stable narrow-complex tachycardia (SVT) → Failed vagal maneuvers → IV Adenosine 6mg rapid push (can repeat 12mg x2). If unstable (hypotension, AMS) → synchronized cardioversion. Amiodarone for wide-complex or refractory SVT.

text: A family physician is invited to a pharmaceutical company-sponsored 'lunch and learn' about a new antihypertensive. The company offers to pay travel expenses and provides a free lunch. According to Canadian ethical guidelines, what is the most appropriate action?
options: Accept travel and lunch if the physician agrees to prescribe the medication to appropriate patients.
Decline the sponsorship for travel expenses, as it is inappropriate unless the physician is a presenter.
Accept the lunch and travel but disclose this to all patients receiving the medication.
Allow the company to review prescribing data in exchange for the benefits.
rationale: CMA Code of Ethics: Travel sponsorship to attend (not present at) industry events is INAPPROPRIATE. Modest meals at educational events may be acceptable. Never accept gifts/benefits that could influence prescribing. Never share patient prescribing data.

text: A 29-year-old postpartum patient who received epidural for labor analgesia reports severe headache on day 2. Headache worsens significantly when sitting/standing and almost completely resolves when lying flat. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Post-Dural Puncture Headache (PDPH): Classic postural headache - worse upright, better supine. Occurs 6-72 hrs post epidural/spinal. Due to CSF leak → decreased intracranial pressure. Tx: conservative (fluids, caffeine, analgesics) or epidural blood patch.

text: In a randomized controlled trial, the case fatality rate was lower in patients who received a drug. The result reported P = 0.05. What does this mean?
options: Treated patients were 20 times less likely to die
The probability that the observed difference occurred by chance alone is 1 in 20
The same result will occur in 19 out of 20 trials
Ninety-five percent of treated patients survived
rationale: P=0.05 means 5% probability (1 in 20) the result occurred by chance. It does NOT mean 95% of patients survived or results replicate 19/20 times. If P<0.05, results are statistically significant.

text: A 25-year-old man presents with 2 months of knee swelling and pain, plus 2 years of low back pain and morning stiffness. Which investigation would most likely confirm the diagnosis?
rationale: Dx: Ankylosing Spondylitis (AS). Young male + chronic low back pain + morning stiffness + peripheral arthritis. Key finding: sacroiliitis on X-ray/MRI of sacroiliac joints. HLA-B27 positive in 90%. Tx: NSAIDs, physiotherapy, anti-TNF if refractory.

text: A woman is found unconscious in her car in a closed garage. Her PaO2 is normal, but pulse oximetry shows low oxygen saturation. What explains this discrepancy?
rationale: CO poisoning: CO binds hemoglobin 240x stronger than O2 → carboxyhemoglobin. PaO2 = NORMAL (dissolved O2 unchanged), but pulse oximetry falsely reads carboxyhemoglobin as oxyhemoglobin → falsely LOW SpO2. Tx: 100% O2, hyperbaric O2 if severe.

text: A 77-year-old man reports urinary frequency, nocturia, and feeling that his bladder is always full, with leakage of urine day and night. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Overflow incontinence: Bladder cannot empty (BPH most common cause in elderly men) → chronic retention → overflow leakage. Hallmark: sensation of bladder always full + continuous leakage. Dx: post-void residual > 300mL. Tx: alpha-blockers, catheterization, TURP.

text: An 8-year-old boy has a persistent barking cough for 6 months. His mother reports frequent blinking and head shaking movements. Which aspect of the patient's history is most important?
rationale: Dx: Tourette Syndrome = motor tics (blinking, head shaking) + vocal tics (barking cough). Most important: school/academic performance - Tourette often associated with ADHD, OCD, learning difficulties. Must assess impact on daily functioning.

text: A woman reports physical abuse by her husband. A police officer requests medical information for the investigation. What is the best next step?
options: Review the patient's file and speak with police
Contact the patient to discuss the request
Send a medical report to police
rationale: Patient confidentiality: ALWAYS get patient consent before releasing medical information to police (unless mandatory reporting e.g. child abuse, gunshot wounds). Contact patient first, explain the request, obtain consent, then share information.

text: A pregnant woman with BMI 58 asks how much weight she should gain during pregnancy. What is the best advice?
options: Weight gain should be based on prepregnancy BMI
rationale: IOM Guidelines: Normal BMI 18.5-24.9 = gain 11.5-16kg. Overweight BMI 25-29.9 = gain 7-11.5kg. Obese BMI >30 = gain 5-9kg. BMI 58 (Class III obese) = minimal weight gain. NEVER calorie-restrict in pregnancy - risk of ketoacidosis.

text: A patient taking paroxetine develops anejaculation and wants to switch antidepressants. Which medication is most appropriate?
rationale: SSRI sexual side effects (anejaculation, decreased libido, anorgasmia) = very common. Bupropion (NDRI) has MINIMAL sexual side effects + may improve sexual function. Other SSRIs/SNRIs would cause same problem. Clomipramine is tricyclic - worse sexual SE.

text: A patient reports: unstable relationships, fear of abandonment, emotional instability, and chronic feelings of emptiness. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Borderline PD: MNEMONIC 'PRAISE': Paranoid ideation, Rage, Abandonment fear, Impulsivity, Self-harm, Empty feelings. Emotional instability + unstable relationships. Tx: DBT (Dialectical Behavior Therapy) is first-line.

text: A teacher believes coworkers are sending secret messages about him through hand signals. He has no hallucinations and maintains normal functioning. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Delusional disorder: fixed false beliefs (delusions) WITHOUT hallucinations or disorganized behavior. Normal functioning preserved. Types: persecutory (most common), referential (ideas of reference), grandiose, erotomanic. Unlike schizophrenia: no other psychotic symptoms.

text: A patient receives a penicillin injection and 30 minutes later develops: BP 70/30, HR 130, wheezing, and flushed skin. What is the most appropriate treatment?
rationale: Anaphylaxis = EPINEPHRINE FIRST (IM, lateral thigh, 0.3-0.5mg 1:1000). Then: IV fluids, antihistamines (H1+H2), corticosteroids. Salbutamol for bronchospasm if persists. Epinephrine reverses vasodilation, bronchoconstriction, and angioedema simultaneously.

text: In a randomized controlled trial evaluating a new drug, the mortality rate in the treated group is lower than in the control group. The result is reported as P = 0.05. What does this indicate?
options: The difference observed could have occurred by chance with a probability of 5%
The treatment will work in 19 of 20 patients
rationale: P=0.05 = 5% probability the result occurred by chance. NOT that treatment works 95% of the time. P<0.05 = statistically significant. P value does NOT measure clinical significance.

text: A 25-year-old man presents with knee swelling and pain for 2 months plus chronic back pain and morning stiffness for 2 years. Which investigation confirms the diagnosis?
rationale: Ankylosing Spondylitis: Young male + chronic low back pain + morning stiffness > 1hr + peripheral arthritis. Dx: sacroiliitis on X-ray/MRI. HLA-B27 in 90%. Tx: NSAIDs first-line, anti-TNF if refractory.

text: A patient is found unconscious in a car inside a closed garage. Arterial oxygen pressure (PaO2) is normal, but pulse oximetry shows low oxygen saturation. Most likely cause?
rationale: CO poisoning: CO binds Hgb forming carboxyhemoglobin. PaO2 = NORMAL (dissolved O2 fine). Pulse oximetry falsely reads carboxyhemoglobin as oxyhemoglobin = falsely LOW SpO2. Tx: 100% O2 via NRB mask, hyperbaric O2 if severe/pregnant.

text: A 77-year-old man reports urinary frequency, nocturia, persistent bladder fullness with leakage. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Overflow incontinence: bladder cannot empty (BPH in elderly men) → chronic retention → overflow leakage. Key: sensation of always full bladder. Post-void residual >300mL. Tx: alpha-blockers (tamsulosin), 5-alpha reductase inhibitors, TURP.

text: An 8-year-old boy has frequent blinking, head shaking, and repeated coughing sounds for several months. Which condition is most likely?
rationale: Tourette Syndrome: motor tics (blinking, head shaking) + vocal tics (coughing, throat clearing) >1 year, onset <18yo. Comorbidities: ADHD (50%), OCD (40%). Tx: behavioral therapy, clonidine, haloperidol for severe tics.

text: A woman reports physical abuse by her partner. Police request medical records. What is the best next step?
options: Transfer the case to social services
rationale: Confidentiality: Contact patient FIRST, explain police request, obtain CONSENT before releasing any information. Exception: mandatory reporting (child abuse, gunshot wounds, certain communicable diseases). Domestic violence alone does NOT require mandatory reporting.

text: A pregnant woman with BMI 58 asks about recommended weight gain during pregnancy. What is the best recommendation?
options: Base weight gain on prepregnancy BMI
rationale: IOM Weight Gain Guidelines: Underweight (<18.5) = 12.5-18kg. Normal (18.5-24.9) = 11.5-16kg. Overweight (25-29.9) = 7-11.5kg. Obese (>30) = 5-9kg. BMI 58 = gain only 5-9kg. NEVER calorie-restrict in pregnancy.

text: A patient taking paroxetine develops sexual dysfunction. Which antidepressant is least likely to cause this side effect?
rationale: Bupropion (NDRI) = minimal/no sexual SE. SSRIs (paroxetine, fluoxetine, sertraline) = sexual dysfunction in 30-40%. SNRIs (venlafaxine) = similar. Mirtazapine also low sexual SE. Clomipramine (TCA) = worst sexual SE.

text: A patient reports fear of abandonment, unstable relationships, impulsivity, and emotional instability. Diagnosis?
rationale: Borderline PD (PRAISE mnemonic): Paranoia, Rage, Abandonment fear, Identity disturbance, Self-harm/Suicidality, Emptiness. Tx: DBT (Dialectical Behavior Therapy) = first-line. Antisocial PD = no remorse/empathy. Narcissistic PD = grandiosity.

text: A teacher believes coworkers communicate secret messages about him using hand gestures. No hallucinations, normal functioning. Diagnosis?
rationale: Delusional disorder: fixed delusions (referential type here) WITHOUT hallucinations or disorganized behavior. Normal functioning preserved. Unlike schizophrenia: no other psychotic symptoms. Tx: antipsychotics, CBT.

text: A child develops fever, drooling, and stridor. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Epiglottitis: 'Toxic tripod' = drooling + dysphagia + distress. Sudden onset, high fever, stridor. Caused by H. influenzae type B (now rare with HiB vaccine). EMERGENCY: Do NOT examine throat, do NOT lay flat. Tx: secure airway, IV ceftriaxone.

text: A 4-month-old infant develops cough and wheezing after a viral upper respiratory infection. Most likely cause?
rationale: RSV (Respiratory Syncytial Virus) = #1 cause of bronchiolitis in infants <2yo. Winter months. Wheezing + cough + tachypnea after URI. Dx: clinical. Tx: supportive (O2, fluids). Prophylaxis: Palivizumab for high-risk infants.

text: A patient develops hypotension, wheezing, and rash after penicillin injection. Best treatment?
rationale: Anaphylaxis: EPINEPHRINE FIRST (IM, anterolateral thigh, 0.3-0.5mg 1:1000). Then: IV fluids, H1+H2 antihistamines, corticosteroids, salbutamol for bronchospasm. Epi works immediately; steroids take hours.

text: A patient with severe pancreatitis develops dyspnea and dullness to percussion on the left chest. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Pancreatitis → reactive left-sided pleural effusion (amylase-rich). Dullness = fluid (effusion). Pneumothorax = hyperresonance. Dx: CXR, ultrasound. Also watch for ARDS in severe pancreatitis.

text: A trauma patient develops elevated creatinine after a crush injury. Most likely cause?
rationale: Rhabdomyolysis: crush injury → muscle breakdown → myoglobin release → renal tubular injury → AKI. Classic: brown/tea-colored urine, elevated CK (>1000 U/L), elevated creatinine. Tx: aggressive IV fluids (NS), urine output >300mL/hr.

text: A patient taking alprazolam daily stops suddenly and develops tremor, anxiety, sweating, and agitation. Best treatment?
rationale: Benzodiazepine withdrawal: LIFE-THREATENING (like alcohol withdrawal) → seizures, delirium. Tx: Restart same benzodiazepine, then gradual taper (10% per week). OR switch to long-acting benzo (diazepam) then taper. Never stop abruptly.

text: A pregnant woman with hypertension, proteinuria, headache, and epigastric pain presents at 38 weeks. Which test should be included in evaluation for HELLP syndrome?
rationale: HELLP syndrome: Hemolysis (elevated LDH) + Elevated Liver enzymes (AST/ALT) + Low Platelets. Must check: LDH, CBC, LFTs, peripheral smear. LDH is the key hemolysis marker. Tx: delivery is definitive treatment.

text: A 5-year-old child snores loudly and has daytime fatigue. Which test confirms the diagnosis of obstructive sleep apnea?
rationale: Polysomnography (overnight sleep study) = gold standard for OSA. Measures AHI (apnea-hypopnea index). In children: tonsil/adenoid hypertrophy is #1 cause. Tx: adenotonsillectomy first-line in children.

text: A patient with schizoaffective disorder gains 15 kg after starting an atypical antipsychotic. Best management?
options: Switch to another atypical antipsychotic with fewer metabolic effects
rationale: Metabolic SE of atypicals: Olanzapine and Clozapine = MOST weight gain. Aripiprazole, Ziprasidone, Lurasidone = LEAST weight gain. Switch to lower metabolic-risk antipsychotic. Monitor: weight, fasting glucose, lipids (metabolic syndrome).

text: A patient with ALS has severe pain not controlled by other treatments. Morphine is prescribed. The patient later dies. What is this considered?
rationale: Principle of Double Effect: providing adequate pain relief (even if it may shorten life) is ethical and legal in palliative care. This is NOT euthanasia or assisted suicide. The INTENT is to relieve suffering, not to cause death.

text: A 9-month-old infant is not crawling, can sit only with support, and has mild head lag when pulled to sitting. What is the best next step?
options: Refer to a pediatrician for developmental assessment
rationale: 9-month milestones: sit without support, crawl, pincer grasp emerging. Head lag at 9mo = RED FLAG. Early referral = better outcomes. Never reassure developmental delay without assessment. Rule out cerebral palsy, hypotonia, genetic disorders.

text: A 5-year-old boy repeatedly passes stool in his clothes. Examination shows a firm mass in the left lower quadrant. Most appropriate management?
rationale: Encopresis from chronic constipation: fecal impaction → overflow soiling. LLQ mass = stool. Tx: 1) Disimpaction (laxatives/enema), 2) Maintenance laxatives (PEG), 3) Behavior modification/toilet training. Surgery NOT indicated.

text: A physician studies dermatitis cases among factory workers using workplace records. What type of study is this?
rationale: Descriptive observational study: describes patterns of disease (who, where, when) WITHOUT testing a hypothesis or comparing exposures. No control group. Used for hypothesis generation. Types: case reports, case series, cross-sectional surveys.

text: A child is extremely aggressive, steals, lies, and harms animals. What medication may help control aggression?
rationale: Conduct Disorder: aggression, rule violation, animal cruelty. Risperidone FDA-approved for irritability/aggression in children. Methylphenidate for ADHD comorbidity. Lithium for severe aggression unresponsive to antipsychotics. Lorazepam acute only.

text: A pregnant woman at 30 weeks presents with vaginal bleeding and severe back pain. Best initial step?
rationale: Placental abruption: painful vaginal bleeding + back pain. Priorities: 1) IV access + fluids (stabilize mother), 2) Fetal monitoring, 3) CBC, coag, type+screen. Do NOT do vaginal exam if placenta previa possible. Abruption is emergency.

text: A patient develops polyuria (5.5 L/day) and hypernatremia after pituitary surgery. Best treatment?
rationale: Central Diabetes Insipidus: pituitary surgery → ADH deficiency → dilute polyuria (urine osmol <300) + hypernatremia. Dx: water deprivation test, then desmopressin → urine concentrates. Tx: desmopressin (DDAVP). Nephrogenic DI: hydrochlorothiazide.

text: A 19-year-old man presents with acute scrotal pain and a groin mass extending into the scrotum. Best next step?
rationale: Incarcerated inguinal hernia: bowel trapped, cannot reduce. SURGICAL EMERGENCY to prevent strangulation and bowel necrosis. Unlike uncomplicated hernia (can reduce), incarcerated = urgent OR. Don't delay with ultrasound if diagnosis is clear.

text: A 72-year-old man with COPD develops bilateral leg edema and elevated jugular venous pressure. Which murmur is most likely present?
options: Holosystolic murmur at left lower sternal border
rationale: Cor Pulmonale: COPD → pulmonary HTN → RV failure → tricuspid regurgitation. TR murmur: holosystolic at left lower sternal border (LLSB), increases with inspiration (Carvallo sign). Also: JVP elevated, hepatomegaly, peripheral edema.

text: A woman presents with right upper quadrant pain after fatty meals. Ultrasound is normal but HIDA scan shows ejection fraction of 6%. Best treatment?
rationale: Biliary dyskinesia: normal ultrasound + low gallbladder ejection fraction (<35%) on HIDA scan. Tx: laparoscopic cholecystectomy → 90% symptom relief. NOT PPIs or ERCP. Ursodiol for gallstones, NOT dyskinesia.

text: A patient with a history of abdominal aneurysm repair presents with hematemesis. Best investigation?
rationale: Aortoenteric fistula: rare but life-threatening complication of AAA repair. Graft erodes into duodenum → hematemesis. CT angiography = best initial investigation (shows fistula, graft integrity). Urgent surgical repair required.

text: A bipolar patient stabilized on lithium asks how to prevent relapse. Best recommendation?
rationale: Bipolar relapse prevention: MOST IMPORTANT non-pharmacologic = regular sleep-wake cycle. Sleep disruption is the #1 trigger for mania. Also: avoid alcohol, stress management, medication adherence, psychoeducation. Regular sleep > therapy in relapse prevention.

text: A patient with multiple rib fractures has paradoxical chest wall movement. Best next step after oxygen?
rationale: Flail chest: >3 consecutive ribs fractured in 2+ places → paradoxical movement. Pain → splinting → atelectasis → hypoxia. Tx: 1) O2, 2) Epidural analgesia (reduces pain, improves ventilation), 3) Intubation if respiratory failure. Chest tube only if pneumo/hemothorax.

text: A pregnant woman asks which vaccine should be avoided during pregnancy.
rationale: LIVE vaccines contraindicated in pregnancy: MMR, Varicella, Live attenuated influenza (nasal), Yellow fever (avoid unless high-risk). SAFE in pregnancy: inactivated influenza, Tdap (28-32 weeks), Hepatitis A/B, COVID-19. Tdap every pregnancy.

text: A child has limp hand and absent grasp reflex after birth trauma. Most likely cause?
rationale: Birth brachial plexus injury: Erb palsy (C5-C6) = waiter's tip position, absent Moro arm abduction. Klumpke palsy (C8-T1) = limp hand, absent grasp. Associated with shoulder dystocia, macrosomia. Most recover spontaneously in 3-6 months.

text: A child with high fever and inability to bear weight on one leg presents with localized bone tenderness. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Osteomyelitis: fever + localized bone tenderness + refusal to bear weight. MCC: S. aureus. Dx: MRI (most sensitive), bone scan, elevated ESR/CRP. Tx: IV antibiotics (cloxacillin or cefazolin). Septic arthritis: joint pain, not bone tenderness.

text: A 67-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden onset shortness of breath that started 2 hours ago while watching television. He reports mild chest discomfort. Medical history includes hypertension and recent hip replacement surgery 10 days ago. Vitals: BP 100/60, HR 118, RR 26, SpO2 90%. Physical exam shows tachycardia and mild left calf tenderness. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
rationale: High probability PE: recent surgery + tachycardia + hypoxia + calf tenderness = Wells score HIGH. CT pulmonary angiography = gold standard for high-probability PE. D-dimer is for LOW probability only (high sensitivity, low specificity). Anticoagulate immediately while awaiting CT.

text: A 23-year-old woman presents with lower abdominal pain and vaginal discharge for 3 days. She is sexually active with multiple partners without consistent protection. Pelvic exam shows cervical motion tenderness and purulent discharge. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Observe and repeat exam in 48 hours
Start oral antibiotics for pelvic inflammatory disease
rationale: PID Dx: Cervical motion tenderness + lower abdominal pain + purulent discharge = treat empirically without waiting for cultures. Outpatient Tx: Cefixime 250mg IM + Doxycycline 100mg BID x14d + Metronidazole. Admit only if: pregnant, surgical emergency, failed outpatient tx, unable to tolerate PO.

text: A 72-year-old man presents with difficulty starting urination and weak urinary stream for the past year. He also reports nocturia and incomplete bladder emptying. Digital rectal exam reveals a smooth, symmetrically enlarged prostate. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: BPH: LUTS in elderly male + smooth symmetric enlarged prostate = BPH. Prostate cancer = hard, irregular, nodular prostate. Prostatitis = tender prostate + fever. Dx: IPSS score + post-void residual. Tx: alpha-blockers (tamsulosin), 5-alpha reductase inhibitors (finasteride), or TURP.

text: A 6-year-old boy is brought to the clinic with fever, drooling, and difficulty breathing. He appears anxious and prefers to sit leaning forward. Stridor is present. What is the most appropriate next step?
rationale: Epiglottitis: Drooling + Dysphagia + Distress (tripod position) + Stridor = EMERGENCY. NEVER examine throat (may cause complete obstruction). Priority = Airway first (intubation in OR by experienced team). Then: IV ceftriaxone. X-ray/cultures are secondary.

text: A 35-year-old woman presents with fatigue, cold intolerance, and weight gain. Physical exam shows dry skin, bradycardia, and puffy face. Labs show elevated TSH and low Free T4. What is the best initial treatment?
rationale: Primary Hypothyroidism: High TSH + Low T4 = primary hypothyroidism. Tx: Levothyroxine (synthetic T4), titrate to normalize TSH. Methimazole = hyperthyroidism. Propranolol = symptomatic hyperthyroidism. Radioiodine = Graves disease. Monitor TSH q6-8wks after dose change.

text: A 58-year-old man presents with sudden severe headache described as the worst headache of his life. He reports neck stiffness and vomiting. What is the first investigation?
options: CT scan of the head without contrast
rationale: SAH: Thunderclap headache (worst of life) + meningismus = SAH until proven otherwise. Sequence: 1) CT head without contrast (sensitive in first 12hr, 98%) → 2) If CT negative: LP (xanthochromia confirms SAH). MRI less sensitive for acute SAH. Do NOT do LP first if CT not done (risk of herniation).

text: A 45-year-old woman presents with episodic palpitations, sweating, and headache. Blood pressure during attacks is 210/120 mmHg. What condition should be suspected?
rationale: Pheochromocytoma: Classic TRIAD = Episodic headache + Sweating + Palpitations + Hypertensive crises. Dx: 24hr urine metanephrines or plasma free metanephrines (most sensitive). Imaging: CT/MRI adrenal. Tx: Alpha-blocker FIRST (phenoxybenzamine), then beta-blocker, then surgery.

text: A 3-year-old child presents with barking cough and inspiratory stridor. Symptoms worsen at night. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Croup (Laryngotracheobronchitis): Age 6mo-3yr + barking/seal-like cough + inspiratory stridor + worse at night + MCC = Parainfluenza virus. CXR: Steeple sign. Tx: Dexamethasone 0.6mg/kg PO (mild/mod) + Nebulized epinephrine (severe). Epiglottitis = drooling + toxic + no barking.

text: A 70-year-old woman falls and sustains a hip fracture. What is the most important complication to prevent during hospitalization?
rationale: DVT/PE prophylaxis: Hip fracture = very high VTE risk. ALL patients require pharmacologic thromboprophylaxis: LMWH (enoxaparin) or fondaparinux for at least 35 days. Also: early mobilization, compression stockings. Without prophylaxis: DVT risk up to 50%, fatal PE risk 0.1-2%.

text: A patient with diabetes presents with confusion, dehydration, and blood glucose 35 mmol/L. No ketones are detected. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: HHS vs DKA: HHS = glucose >33 mmol/L + NO/minimal ketones + severe dehydration + altered consciousness. Usually type 2 DM. Osmolality >320. DKA = ketones + metabolic acidosis + type 1 DM. Tx HHS: Aggressive IV fluids (NS) + insulin infusion + electrolyte replacement. Mortality 10-20%.

text: A 68-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden weakness of the right arm and difficulty speaking that started 45 minutes ago. Medical history includes hypertension and atrial fibrillation. Vital signs are stable. What is the most appropriate next step?
rationale: Acute stroke protocol: CT head without contrast FIRST to rule out hemorrhagic stroke before giving thrombolytics (tPA). MRI is more sensitive but slower. Aspirin is given AFTER hemorrhage excluded. Window for tPA: 4.5 hours. AF = cardioembolic source → anticoagulation long-term.

text: A 72-year-old woman presents with gradual onset memory loss over the past 2 years. She has difficulty remembering recent events and frequently misplaces objects. Neurologic exam is normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Alzheimer Disease: MC dementia (60-80%). Insidious onset + progressive + episodic memory loss first. Delirium = acute/fluctuating. Depression = pseudodementia (treat → improves). Vascular dementia = stepwise decline + vascular risk factors. Dx: Clinical + MoCA/MMSE. No cure; treat with donepezil/memantine.

text: A 24-year-old woman presents with acute right lower quadrant pain, nausea, and fever. Physical examination shows rebound tenderness at McBurney point. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Acute Appendicitis: RLQ pain + fever + nausea + rebound at McBurney point. Alvarado score helps. Dx: CT abdomen (best). Tx: Appendectomy (laparoscopic). In females: always rule out ectopic pregnancy (β-hCG) and PID. Rovsing sign / psoas sign support Dx.

text: A 65-year-old smoker presents with painless hematuria. What diagnosis must be ruled out?
rationale: Bladder cancer: Painless hematuria in smoker >50 = bladder cancer until proven otherwise. Risk factors: Smoking #1, aniline dyes, cyclophosphamide. Dx: Cystoscopy + biopsy (gold standard). CT urogram for upper tract. MC type: transitional cell carcinoma.

text: A patient presents with burning chest pain after meals and when lying down. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: GERD: Heartburn + worse lying down + worse after meals + regurgitation. Tx: Lifestyle (HOB elevation, avoid triggers) + PPI first-line. Alarm features (dysphagia, weight loss, bleeding) → endoscopy. Complication: Barrett esophagus → esophageal adenocarcinoma.

text: A 60-year-old man presents with shortness of breath, orthopnea, and bilateral leg edema. On exam there are bilateral crackles in the lungs. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Congestive Heart Failure: Dyspnea + orthopnea + bilateral crackles + peripheral edema = classic CHF. Dx: BNP/NT-proBNP elevated, CXR (cardiomegaly, Kerley B lines, bat-wing pattern), Echo (EF). Tx HFrEF: ACEi/ARB + beta-blocker + diuretics (furosemide) + spironolactone.

text: A patient presents with sudden severe unilateral headache with tearing and nasal congestion. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Cluster Headache: Severe unilateral orbital/periorbital pain + autonomic features (tearing, nasal congestion, Horner syndrome). Duration 15-180 min, clusters over weeks. M>F. Acute Tx: 100% O2 + sumatriptan SC. Prevention: verapamil. NOT bilateral (unlike tension). NOT aura (unlike migraine).

text: A 30-year-old woman presents with heat intolerance, tremor, and weight loss. TSH is low. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Hyperthyroidism: Low TSH + high T4 + heat intolerance + tremor + weight loss + tachycardia. MC cause: Graves disease (+ exophthalmos + pretibial myxedema). Dx: TSH + free T4 + RAIU scan. Tx: Methimazole (propylthiouracil in pregnancy) → radioiodine or surgery.

text: A newborn develops jaundice within the first 12 hours of life. What is the most likely cause?
rationale: Neonatal Jaundice timing: <24hrs = PATHOLOGIC = hemolytic (Rh/ABO incompatibility, G6PD). Day 2-7 = physiologic. Day 5-14 = breast milk jaundice. Tx: Phototherapy. Exchange transfusion if severe. Always pathologic if <24hr → investigate urgently.

text: A 45-year-old man presents with severe epigastric pain radiating to the back. Lipase level is elevated. What is the diagnosis?
rationale: Acute Pancreatitis: Epigastric pain + radiates to back + elevated lipase (>3x normal) = pancreatitis. MC causes: Gallstones (GET SMASHED mnemonic: G=Gallstones, E=EtOH). Severity: Ranson criteria or CT severity index. Tx: NPO + IV fluids + analgesia. Grey-Turner/Cullen signs = hemorrhagic.

text: A 20-year-old athlete presents with sudden severe testicular pain. Physical exam shows a high-riding testicle. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Testicular Torsion: SURGICAL EMERGENCY. Sudden severe pain + high-riding testicle + absent cremasteric reflex. Window: 6 hrs (100% salvage) → 12 hrs (50%) → 24 hrs (<10%). Dx: Doppler US (absent flow) but don't delay surgery. Tx: Emergency surgical detorsion + bilateral orchiopexy. Epididymitis = gradual onset + fever + Prehn sign positive.

text: A patient presents with fatigue, pallor, and microcytic anemia. What is the most common cause?
rationale: Iron Deficiency Anemia: MC anemia worldwide. Microcytic (MCV<80) + hypochromic + low ferritin + high TIBC. Causes: blood loss (GI #1 in men/postmenopausal women, menorrhagia in women), poor intake. Tx: Oral ferrous sulfate. Always investigate the SOURCE of blood loss.

text: A patient presents with chronic cough, night sweats, and weight loss. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Tuberculosis: Classic triad = chronic cough + night sweats + weight loss. Risk: immigrant, HIV, crowded conditions. CXR: upper lobe cavitation. Dx: sputum AFB smear/culture + IGRA/TST. Tx: RIPE x2mo → RI x4mo (Rifampin + Isoniazid + Pyrazinamide + Ethambutol). ISOLATE the patient.

text: A patient presents with the worst headache of life and vomiting. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: SAH: Thunderclap headache = SAH until proven otherwise. Caused by ruptured berry aneurysm. Sentinel bleed may precede rupture. MCCQE sequence: CT head (no contrast) → if negative → LP (xanthochromia). Tx: Nimodipine (vasospasm prevention) + neurosurgical consultation.

text: A 3-year-old child presents with barking cough and inspiratory stridor. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Croup (Viral Laryngotracheobronchitis): Age 6mo-3yr + barking/seal cough + inspiratory stridor + viral prodrome + worse at night. MCC: Parainfluenza virus. CXR: steeple sign. Tx: Dexamethasone 0.6mg/kg PO/IM (all) + nebulized epinephrine (moderate-severe, ED). Epiglottitis = drooling + toxic + NO barking.

text: A patient develops hypotension, wheezing, and rash after receiving penicillin. What is the first treatment?
rationale: Anaphylaxis: Epinephrine IM (0.3-0.5mg 1:1000) into lateral thigh = FIRST LINE. Then: O2 + IV fluids + antihistamine + steroids (prevent biphasic). Mechanism: IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation. Observe 4-6hrs (biphasic reaction). Prescribe EpiPen on discharge. Steroids and antihistamines are ADJUNCTS only.

text: A patient presents with joint pain and morning stiffness lasting over 1 hour. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Rheumatoid Arthritis: Morning stiffness >1hr + symmetric small joint involvement (MCP, PIP) + RF/anti-CCP positive. OA = morning stiffness <30min + DIP/PIP + worse with activity. Gout = acute monoarthritis + urate crystals. Tx RA: MTX (DMARD first-line) + NSAIDs/steroids for flares + biologics (anti-TNF).

text: A patient presents with polyuria and polydipsia. Blood glucose is 30 mmol/L. What is the diagnosis?
rationale: Diabetes Mellitus: Hyperglycemia (>11.1 mmol/L random or >7 fasting) + polyuria/polydipsia. DI = dilute urine + normal glucose + high serum osmolality. Dx: HbA1c ≥6.5% + fasting glucose ≥7.0. Tx Type 2: Metformin first. Type 1: Insulin.

text: A patient presents with severe abdominal pain and rigid abdomen. What is the most likely cause?
rationale: Perforated Peptic Ulcer: Board-like rigid abdomen = peritonitis = perforation. Sudden onset severe pain, patient lies still (peritoneal irritation). CXR: free air under diaphragm. Tx: Emergent surgical repair + IV PPI + broad-spectrum antibiotics. Risk factors: NSAIDs + H.pylori.

text: A patient presents with progressive dysphagia first to solids then liquids. What is the most concerning diagnosis?
rationale: Esophageal Cancer: Progressive dysphagia solids→liquids + weight loss = cancer until proven otherwise. MC types: SCC (upper/mid, smoking + alcohol) vs Adenocarcinoma (lower, Barrett's/GERD). Achalasia = difficulty solids AND liquids from start + bird-beak on barium. Dx: Endoscopy + biopsy.

text: A 28-year-old woman presents with shortness of breath, pleuritic chest pain, and tachycardia 1 week after cesarean delivery. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: PE post-partum: C-section + postpartum = highest VTE risk. Virchow's triad: stasis + hypercoagulability + vessel injury. Wells score likely HIGH. Tx: LMWH (enoxaparin). CT-PA = gold standard Dx. D-dimer unreliable postpartum (elevated baseline).

text: A 74-year-old man with COPD presents with increased dyspnea, purulent sputum, and wheezing. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: COPD Exacerbation: Worsening dyspnea + increased sputum + purulence (Anthonisen criteria ≥2 = moderate exacerbation). Tx: SABA + SAMA (ipratropium) + prednisone 40mg x5d + antibiotics if purulent (amoxicillin or doxycycline). O2 target: 88-92%.

text: A 19-year-old woman presents with missed periods, weight loss, and excessive exercise. What is the most likely cause of her amenorrhea?
rationale: Functional Hypothalamic Amenorrhea: Low body weight + excessive exercise + stress = GnRH suppression → low LH/FSH → low estrogen → amenorrhea. Female Athlete Triad: disordered eating + amenorrhea + osteoporosis. Always rule out pregnancy first (β-hCG).

text: A 55-year-old man presents with crushing chest pain. ECG shows inferior STEMI. Which leads are most likely elevated?
rationale: Inferior MI: Leads II, III, aVF = RCA territory (80%) or LCx (20%). Look for right-sided leads (V3R, V4R) to rule out RV infarction. RV MI → avoid nitrates (preload dependent). Tx: Primary PCI within 90min (door-to-balloon). Aspirin + heparin + P2Y12 inhibitor.

text: A 30-year-old woman presents with episodic right upper quadrant pain after fatty meals. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Biliary Colic: RUQ pain after fatty meals = gallbladder contracts against obstructing stone. Pain episodic, lasts 30min-6hr, resolves spontaneously. Dx: US abdomen (best). Tx: Elective cholecystectomy. Complications: cholecystitis (fever + Murphy sign), cholangitis (Charcot's triad: fever + jaundice + RUQ pain).

text: A 6-year-old child presents with fever, swollen cervical nodes, conjunctivitis, cracked lips, and rash. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Kawasaki Disease: Fever >5 days + 4 of 5 criteria: Conjunctivitis (bilateral, non-purulent) + Rash (polymorphous) + Adenopathy (cervical) + Strawberry tongue/cracked lips + Extremity changes. Dx risk: Coronary artery aneurysms. Tx: IVIG + high-dose ASA. Echo mandatory.

text: A patient presents with sudden onset painful red eye, blurred vision, and halos around lights. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Acute Angle-Closure Glaucoma: EMERGENCY. Painful red eye + halos + blurred vision + fixed mid-dilated pupil + firm eye. Precipitated by mydriatics, dim light. Tx: IV acetazolamide + topical timolol + pilocarpine + urgent ophthalmology. Conjunctivitis = no pain, no halos.

text: A 48-year-old man presents with weight loss, tremor, palpitations, and lid lag. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Hyperthyroidism: Lid lag (Von Graefe sign) + lid retraction + exophthalmos = Graves disease. Low TSH + high free T4. Tx: Methimazole (or PTU in pregnancy/thyroid storm). Radioiodine for definitive. Propranolol for symptom control. Thyroid storm: antithyroids + beta-blocker + steroids + iodine.

text: A patient presents with fever, dysuria, and flank pain. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Acute Pyelonephritis: Dysuria + fever + CVA (costovertebral angle) tenderness = upper UTI. UA: WBC casts = pathognomonic. Blood cultures if septic. Tx outpatient: Cefixime or fluoroquinolone x7-14d. Hospitalize if: vomiting, sepsis, pregnancy, failed outpatient. IV ceftriaxone inpatient.

text: A 9-year-old boy presents with polyuria, weight loss, abdominal pain, and vomiting. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Pediatric DKA: Polyuria + polydipsia + weight loss + abdominal pain + vomiting = DKA until proven otherwise in child. ABG: metabolic acidosis + ketones. Glucose >11 mmol/L. Tx: IV fluids (cautious in peds - cerebral edema risk) + insulin infusion + electrolyte replacement. Monitor K+ closely.

text: A patient presents with sudden unilateral leg pain, pallor, pulselessness, and paresthesia. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Acute Limb Ischemia: 6 P's: Pain + Pallor + Pulselessness + Paresthesia + Paralysis + Poikilothermia. Surgical EMERGENCY (6hr window). Causes: embolus (AF) or thrombosis. Dx: CT angiography. Tx: Heparin immediately + urgent revascularization (embolectomy or thrombolysis). Paralysis/paresthesia = late signs.

text: A 33-year-old woman presents with fatigue and heavy menstrual bleeding. CBC shows microcytic anemia. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Iron Deficiency from Menorrhagia: Low ferritin + low MCV + high TIBC + heavy periods. Most common anemia in women of reproductive age. Tx: Ferrous sulfate 300mg TID + treat underlying cause (menorrhagia). IV iron if cannot tolerate PO or malabsorption. Recheck CBC in 4-6 weeks.

text: A 42-year-old man presents with epigastric pain radiating to the back and elevated lipase. What is the first-line initial management?
rationale: Acute Pancreatitis Initial Tx: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (Lactated Ringer's preferred over NS) = cornerstone of treatment. NPO + analgesia (opioids). Antibiotics NOT routine (only for infected necrosis). ERCP for gallstone pancreatitis with cholangitis. Surgery for complications (abscess, necrosis). Monitor with Ranson criteria.

text: A patient presents with fever and productive cough. Chest X-ray shows right lower lobe consolidation. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: CAP: Fever + productive cough + lobar consolidation = CAP. MCC: S. pneumoniae (#1). Severity: CURB-65 (Confusion, Urea, RR≥30, BP<90, Age≥65). Score 0-1: outpatient. 2: consider admit. 3+: ICU. Tx outpatient: Amoxicillin OR Azithromycin (if atypical). Inpatient: beta-lactam + macrolide or respiratory fluoroquinolone.

text: A 22-year-old man presents with sudden swelling and pain in the scrotum after exercise. What is the most important diagnosis to exclude?
rationale: Testicular Torsion: ALWAYS exclude first in acute scrotal pain. Don't delay surgery for imaging if suspicion is high. Doppler US: absent blood flow. Salvage rate: 6hr=100%, 12hr=50%, 24hr=<10%. Tx: Emergency surgical detorsion + bilateral orchiopexy. Epididymitis = gradual onset + fever + positive Prehn sign + normal cremasteric reflex.

text: A 70-year-old woman presents with new onset unilateral temporal headache and jaw claudication. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Giant Cell Arteritis (Temporal Arteritis): Age >50 + unilateral temporal headache + jaw claudication + scalp tenderness + elevated ESR/CRP. EMERGENCY: may cause blindness (anterior ischemic optic neuropathy). Tx: Start high-dose prednisone 1mg/kg IMMEDIATELY, don't wait for biopsy. Biopsy within 2 weeks (still positive). Temporal artery biopsy = gold standard.

text: A patient presents with progressive shortness of breath and pleuritic chest pain. CXR is normal. What diagnosis should still be strongly considered?
rationale: PE with normal CXR: CXR is normal in majority of PE cases. Classic CXR findings (Hampton hump, Westermark sign) are rare. Normal CXR + hypoxia + pleuritic pain = PE until proven otherwise. Wells score + D-dimer/CT-PA is the workup. Never dismiss PE based on normal CXR alone.

text: A patient with chronic alcohol use presents with confusion, ophthalmoplegia, and gait ataxia. What is the best initial treatment?
rationale: Wernicke Encephalopathy: Classic TRIAD = Confusion + Ophthalmoplegia + Ataxia. Caused by thiamine (B1) deficiency in alcoholics. CRITICAL: Give thiamine BEFORE glucose (glucose alone can precipitate Wernicke's). Tx: Thiamine 100-500mg IV. If untreated → Korsakoff psychosis (irreversible anterograde amnesia + confabulation).

text: A 51-year-old woman presents with hot flashes, night sweats, and amenorrhea. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Menopause: 12 months amenorrhea + vasomotor symptoms (hot flashes, night sweats) = menopause (avg age 51-52). Dx: Clinical (FSH elevated, estradiol low if needed). Tx: HRT if no contraindications. Non-hormonal: SSRIs/SNRIs, clonidine, gabapentin. Always exclude pregnancy. Rule out thyroid disease (similar symptoms).

text: A child presents with barking cough and inspiratory stridor at night. What is the best initial treatment?
rationale: Croup Treatment: Dexamethasone 0.6mg/kg PO/IM (single dose) = mainstay for ALL severities. Mild: dexamethasone + home. Moderate: dexamethasone + nebulized epinephrine + observe 2-4hr in ED. Severe: dexamethasone + epinephrine + admission ± intubation. Antibiotics NOT indicated (viral etiology). Humidified air has no evidence.

text: A patient presents with burning urination and frequency without fever or flank pain. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Uncomplicated Cystitis: Dysuria + frequency + urgency WITHOUT fever or flank pain = lower UTI (bladder only). UA: nitrites + leukocyte esterase. Tx women: Nitrofurantoin 100mg BID x5d (first-line) OR TMP-SMX x3d. Avoid fluoroquinolones for uncomplicated UTI (resistance, side effects). Pyelonephritis = fever + CVA tenderness.

text: A patient presents with sudden onset severe chest pain radiating to the back. What is the most dangerous diagnosis to exclude?
rationale: Aortic Dissection: Sudden tearing/ripping chest pain radiating to back = Type A (ascending) until proven otherwise. BP difference between arms. CXR: widened mediastinum. Dx: CT angiography chest (gold standard) or TEE. Tx Type A: Emergency surgery. Tx Type B: BP control (labetalol) + pain management.

text: A 29-year-old woman presents with malar rash, photosensitivity, and joint pain. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: SLE: Malar (butterfly) rash + photosensitivity + arthritis + young woman. Dx: ANA (sensitive, not specific) + anti-dsDNA (specific) + anti-Sm (most specific). SLICC criteria: 11 criteria, need 4+. Tx: Hydroxychloroquine (all patients) + NSAIDs + steroids for flares + immunosuppressants (severe).

text: A patient presents with painless jaundice and weight loss. What is the most concerning diagnosis?
rationale: Pancreatic Cancer: Painless progressive jaundice + weight loss + new-onset DM in elderly = pancreatic head cancer (obstructing bile duct). Courvoisier sign: palpable non-tender gallbladder. Dx: CT abdomen + CA 19-9. Tx: Whipple procedure if resectable (<20%). Poor prognosis: 5yr survival <10%.

text: A patient presents with severe unilateral eye pain, red eye, and decreased vision. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Acute Angle-Closure Glaucoma: Severe unilateral eye pain + red eye + decreased vision + halos + nausea = EMERGENCY. IOP markedly elevated. Fixed mid-dilated pupil. Tx: IV acetazolamide + topical beta-blocker + pilocarpine + urgent laser iridotomy. Conjunctivitis = bilateral, no pain, no vision loss.

text: A 45-year-old man presents with black tarry stools and epigastric discomfort. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Upper GI Bleed: Melena (black tarry stool) = blood digested in upper GI (proximal to ligament of Treitz). Causes: PUD #1, varices, Mallory-Weiss. Hematochezia (bright red) = lower GI. Dx: Upper endoscopy (diagnostic + therapeutic). Initial Tx: IV PPI + resuscitation. Endoscopy within 24hr.

text: A patient with cirrhosis presents with confusion and asterixis. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Hepatic Encephalopathy: Cirrhosis + confusion + asterixis (flapping tremor) = HE. Precipitants: GI bleed, infection, constipation, medications, electrolyte imbalance. Tx: Lactulose (first-line: targets NH3) + Rifaximin (add-on). Treat precipitants. Grade 3-4: ICU. Liver transplant for refractory.

text: A 34-year-old woman presents with recurrent episodes of wheeze, cough, and chest tightness. Symptoms are worse at night and improve with salbutamol. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Asthma: Variable airflow obstruction + bronchodilator reversibility + atopy history. Nocturnal worsening = classic. Spirometry: FEV1/FVC <0.7 + ≥12% improvement post-bronchodilator. COPD = fixed obstruction + smoker + older. Tx: SABA PRN + ICS maintenance. Step up as needed.

text: A 77-year-old woman presents with hip pain after a fall and cannot bear weight. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Hip Fracture in Elderly: Fall + inability to bear weight + externally rotated/shortened leg = hip fracture. X-ray: AP pelvis. If X-ray negative but high suspicion: MRI (most sensitive). Tx: Surgical fixation within 48hr (reduces mortality). DVT prophylaxis mandatory. Displaced femoral neck in elderly → total hip arthroplasty.

text: A patient presents with sudden onset facial droop involving the forehead. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Bell's Palsy vs Stroke: Bell's palsy = LMN lesion = ENTIRE face affected including forehead. Stroke = UMN lesion = forehead spared (bilateral cortical innervation). Cause: HSV-1 reactivation. Tx: Prednisone (within 72hr) + Valacyclovir (if severe) + eye protection. 70-85% recover completely.

text: A 50-year-old man presents with severe retrosternal chest pain worse when lying flat and improved by sitting forward. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Pericarditis: Pleuritic chest pain + relieved by leaning forward + worse supine. ECG: diffuse saddle-shaped ST elevation + PR depression (pathognomonic). Causes: viral (Coxsackie), autoimmune, post-MI (Dressler's). Tx: NSAIDs + Colchicine (reduces recurrence). Avoid NSAIDs in post-MI pericarditis.

text: A child presents with fever, sore throat, and sandpaper rash. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Scarlet Fever: GAS pharyngitis + sandpaper rash (blanches) + Pastia lines (flexor creases) + strawberry tongue + circumoral pallor. Tx: Penicillin VK x10d. Complications without Tx: rheumatic fever, glomerulonephritis. Measles = Koplik spots + maculopapular rash from head down + cough/coryza/conjunctivitis.

text: A patient presents with palpitations, diaphoresis, and episodic hypertension. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Pheochromocytoma: Paroxysmal HTN + headache + sweating + palpitations = classic triad. Dx: 24hr urine metanephrines/catecholamines OR plasma free metanephrines (most sensitive). Imaging: CT/MRI adrenal. Rule of 10%: 10% bilateral, 10% extra-adrenal, 10% malignant. Tx: Alpha-blocker FIRST (phenoxybenzamine) → then beta-blocker → surgery.

text: A 16-year-old girl presents with fatigue and pallor. CBC shows macrocytic anemia. What deficiency is most likely?
rationale: Macrocytic Anemia: MCV >100. Causes: B12 deficiency (neurological sx: subacute combined degeneration) or Folate deficiency (no neuro sx). B12 def: vegan, pernicious anemia (anti-intrinsic factor Ab), ileal disease. Folate def: poor diet, pregnancy, methotrexate. Tx: Replace the deficient vitamin. Hypersegmented neutrophils on smear.

text: A 65-year-old man presents with difficulty initiating urination, weak stream, and nocturia. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: BPH: LUTS in elderly male = BPH. Smooth symmetric prostate on DRE. Dx: IPSS questionnaire + PSA (rule out cancer) + post-void residual. Tx: Alpha-blockers (tamsulosin) for symptom relief. 5-alpha reductase inhibitors (finasteride) reduce prostate size. Surgery (TURP) if refractory or complications (retention, hydronephrosis).

text: A woman presents with vaginal bleeding in early pregnancy and open cervical os. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Types of Abortion: Threatened = bleeding + closed os. Inevitable = bleeding + OPEN os. Incomplete = tissue passed + open os. Complete = all tissue passed + closed os. Missed = no symptoms + fetal demise. Tx Inevitable: expectant/medical (misoprostol)/surgical (D&C). Always Rh status → Rh-immunoglobulin if Rh-negative.

text: A patient presents with chronic constipation and a palpable stool mass in the left lower quadrant. What is the best initial management?
rationale: Functional Constipation: PEG (polyethylene glycol) laxatives = first-line safe effective treatment. Lifestyle: fiber + fluid + exercise. Stimulant laxatives (senna) for rescue. Colonoscopy if: age >50, rectal bleeding, weight loss, family hx CRC (rule out organic cause). Enemas alone are inadequate for chronic constipation.

text: A patient presents with sudden vertigo, hearing loss, and tinnitus. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Ménière Disease: Episodic vertigo + sensorineural hearing loss + tinnitus + aural fullness. Caused by endolymphatic hydrops. Episodes last 20min-12hr. Dx: Clinical + audiometry. Tx: Low-salt diet + diuretics (hydrochlorothiazide) + antihistamines/antiemetics for acute. BPPV = positional (Dix-Hallpike +). Vestibular neuritis = vertigo only, no hearing loss.

text: A patient presents with recurrent panic attacks and persistent fear of more attacks. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Panic Disorder: Recurrent UNEXPECTED panic attacks + anticipatory anxiety (fear of future attacks) + behavior change. Must rule out organic causes (thyroid, cardiac, pheo). Tx: SSRI/SNRI (first-line long-term) + CBT (most effective). Benzodiazepines PRN only (short-term). Avoid long-term benzos (dependence).

text: A patient presents with fatigue, weight gain, constipation, and dry skin. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Hypothyroidism: Classic: fatigue + weight gain + cold intolerance + constipation + dry skin + bradycardia + myxedema. High TSH + low free T4. Causes: Hashimoto's (anti-TPO Ab) #1, iodine deficiency worldwide. Tx: Levothyroxine, titrate to normalize TSH. Myxedema coma: IV T4 + steroids + supportive.

text: A child presents with intermittent abdominal pain and currant jelly stool. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Intussusception: Classic triad: episodic crampy abdominal pain + vomiting + currant jelly stool (blood + mucus). Peak age 6mo-2yr. Palpable sausage-shaped RUQ mass. Dx: US (target sign) = gold standard for Dx. Tx: Air/contrast enema (reduction) = first-line. Surgery if failed or perforated.

text: A 72-year-old man presents with new resting tremor, rigidity, and bradykinesia. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Parkinson Disease: Cardinal features: TRAP = Tremor (resting, pill-rolling) + Rigidity (cogwheel) + Akinesia/Bradykinesia + Postural instability. Essential tremor = action tremor, improves with alcohol. Dx: Clinical. Tx: Levodopa/carbidopa (most effective), dopamine agonists, MAO-B inhibitors. DBS for refractory.

text: A patient presents with sudden painful swollen calf after a long flight. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: DVT after Immobility: Prolonged immobility (flight, bedrest) + calf swelling + pain + redness = DVT. Wells score for DVT. Dx: Compression US (diagnostic). D-dimer if low probability (high NPV). Tx: LMWH or DOAC (rivaroxaban, apixaban). Warfarin if DOAC contraindicated. Duration: 3-6 months. IVC filter only if anticoagulation contraindicated.

text: A patient presents with epigastric pain worse with meals and weight loss. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Gastric vs Duodenal Ulcer: Gastric ulcer = pain WORSE with meals + weight loss (fear of eating). Duodenal ulcer = pain relieved by food + occurs 2-3hr after meals + nocturnal pain. MCC both: H. pylori + NSAIDs. Dx: Endoscopy (biopsy gastric ulcers to exclude malignancy). Tx: PPI + H. pylori eradication if positive.

text: A 26-year-old woman presents with diffuse abdominal pain, diarrhea, and weight loss for months. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Crohn Disease: Transmural inflammation, any part of GI (mouth to anus), skip lesions, cobblestone. Features: RLQ pain + diarrhea (non-bloody usually) + weight loss + fistulas + perianal disease + extraintestinal (uveitis, arthritis, erythema nodosum). CRP/ESR elevated. Dx: Colonoscopy + biopsy (granulomas). Tx: Steroids → immunomodulators (azathioprine) → biologics (infliximab).

text: A patient presents with fever, dysuria, perineal pain, and tender boggy prostate. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Acute Bacterial Prostatitis: Fever + dysuria + perineal/pelvic pain + tender BOGGY prostate on DRE (do NOT massage - bacteremia risk). MCC: E. coli. Tx: Fluoroquinolone (ciprofloxacin) x4-6wks OR TMP-SMX. Hospitalize if septic (IV antibiotics). Chronic prostatitis = same symptoms but >3 months, afebrile.

text: A child presents with fever, cough, and wheeze after a viral prodrome. What is the most likely diagnosis in an infant?
rationale: Bronchiolitis: Infants <2yr + viral URI prodrome + wheeze + crackles + respiratory distress = bronchiolitis. MCC: RSV (Oct-March). Diagnosis: Clinical. CXR: hyperinflation + patchy infiltrates. Tx: Supportive ONLY (O2, hydration, suctioning). No bronchodilators/steroids/antibiotics routinely. Severe: ICU, intubation. Asthma diagnosed only if recurrent episodes.

text: A patient presents with progressive proximal muscle weakness and heliotrope rash. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Dermatomyositis: Heliotrope rash (periorbital violaceous) + Gottron papules (knuckles) + proximal muscle weakness. Anti-Jo-1 Ab. Elevated CK. Association with malignancy (screen for cancer). EMG: myopathic pattern. Biopsy: inflammatory infiltrate. Tx: High-dose corticosteroids + steroid-sparing agents (azathioprine, methotrexate).

text: A patient presents with new headache, scalp tenderness, and elevated ESR. What is the best initial treatment?
rationale: Giant Cell Arteritis (GCA): START HIGH-DOSE STEROIDS IMMEDIATELY (prednisone 1mg/kg/d) - DO NOT wait for biopsy. Risk: Anterior ischemic optic neuropathy → permanent blindness. Biopsy within 1-2 weeks (still positive after starting steroids). ESR >50 + CRP elevated. Treat for 1-2 years with slow taper. Temporal artery biopsy = gold standard Dx.

text: A patient presents with sudden onset dyspnea, unilateral absent breath sounds, and hyperresonance. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Pneumothorax: Hyperresonance + absent BS + tracheal deviation AWAY (tension) = pneumothorax. Tension PTX = trachea deviates AWAY + hypotension + JVD = EMERGENCY needle decompression 2nd ICS MCL. Simple PTX: if small + stable → observation. If >20% or symptomatic → chest tube. Pleural effusion = DULL to percussion.
